name: Re-anchor on the best measured PTX surface under the current workload

summary.name: Re-anchor on the best measured PTX surface under the current workload
summary.hypothesis: The current workload has drifted enough that the old historical accepted base is no longer a valid recovery target. Restoring the slightly faster 20260421_113859_bf16_gemm_v1_6668d21 PTX variant should establish a sane current-workload anchor before more exploits are layered on top.
summary.expected_bottleneck: Current-workload re-anchoring step; establishes the local PTX baseline before the next bounded barrier exploit.

score_breakdown.reason: Fastest path to a valid current-workload base after the historical restore failed to recover.

ranking_notes: This is not a search breakthrough; it is a workload-aware re-anchor.
The last two measured PTX variants differ by only 0.14 ms, but only one should serve as the next local base.
Use this to reset the loop onto the best current-workload surface before barrier tuning.
summary.stop_condition: Stop if the restored current-workload anchor does not reproduce within noise of the prior 46.3667 ms measurement.

target_hotspots.explanation: Launch Statistics is carrying metric Registers Per Thread.
Occupancy is carrying metric Achieved Occupancy.

expected_local_changes: Restore the implementation surface from measured commit 6668d2193f6619c3de1cc6000711a62fc1f0fcd8.
Treat this as current-workload anchoring, not as a regression recovery toward the stale 24 ms history.
Preserve workflow/workload support files on HEAD while only restoring compiled implementation code.

guardrail_metrics.reason: Tensor activity is part of the active bottleneck picture and should not drop after the next code edit.
Latency-hiding is already weak; active warps should not regress.
long scoreboard stalls are consuming 6.95% of active warp issue slots.
barrier stalls are consuming 5.57% of active warp issue slots.

notes: Build passed. Node A must measure this implementation next.
